coredata.dc:title: Large Faraday rotation in pyrolysis synthesized carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zefan
Savukov, Igor
Hilty, Christian
coredata.dc:description:
Pyrolysis of citric acid produced carbon dots on the 10 nm size scale, tunable by synthesis time. Magneto-optical spectroscopy revealed a size-dependent Faraday rotation. For particles with optimal synthesis time, the Verdet constant was measured as >300 times larger than that of water on a per-mass basis. Introducing ethylenediamine substrate as a nitrogen dopant in the synthesis resulted in a similar effect. These behaviors are indicative of extended sp2 networks in the particles. A strong wavelength dependence with largest rotation at the shortest measured wavelength of 405 nm was observed. This dependence is explained by a comparison with the theory of electron-photon interactions, which predicts an increase of the Verdet constant with 1/(ω
0
2-ω
2 )2 for an angular frequency ω and an absorption line at ω
0 . The fitting of the analytical function to the measured Faraday rotation indicates a wavelength near 300 nm corresponding to the ω
0 , consistent with an extended electron delocalization. Nanomaterials with large magneto-optical effects have new proposed technological applications, such as in their use as magnetic field sensors. The Faraday rotation is related to spin-induced effects, foremost nuclear spin optical rotation, suggesting further applications of these materials supporting an optical readout of spin states in quantum devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene quantum dots
Faraday rotation
Verdet constant
Magneto-optics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Science Foundation Directorate for Mathematical and Physical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the National Science Foundation (CHE-2108691 to I.S. and CHE-2108822 to C.H.) and the Welch Foundation (A-1658 to C.H.) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Prof. Juha Vaara for useful discussions. Assistance with AFM from Dr. Wilson Serem and TEM from Dr. Sisi Xiang of the Texas A&M University Materials Characterization Facility (RRID: SCR_022202) is greatly appreciated, as well as assistance with Raman spectroscopy from Mr. Justin Smolen of the Texas A&M University Laboratory for Synthetic-Biologic Interactions (RRID: SCR_022287).

Financial support from the National Science Foundation (CHE-2108691 to I.S. and CHE-2108822 to C.H.) and the Welch Foundation (A-1658 to C.H.) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Prof. Juha Vaara for useful discussions. Assistance with AFM from Dr. Wilson Serem and TEM from Dr. Sisi Xiang of the Texas A&M Materials Characterization Facility (RRID: SCR_022202) is greatly appreciated, as well as assistance with Raman from Mr. Justin Smolen.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Instrument for the Faraday rotation measurement: a) Optical configuration and b) opto-electrical diagram. Optical configuration: ① a laser, ② a linear polarizer, ③ a sample cuvette inside a Helmholtz coil, ④ a polarizing beam splitter cube, ⑤ photodiode detectors. Electrical connections are shown in black with open arrows. Two calibration signals, including a single-channel optical signal from the difference amplifier and a reference voltage signal from the audio amplifier, are represented with dashed lines. The laser beam is shown in red with an arrow. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

AFM images of a-d) GQD-45, GQD-60, GQD-75, GQD-90, e-h) their height profiles and i) UV–vis mass extinction coefficients of all GQD and N-GQD aqueous solutions as calculated by Equation S(2). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Unreferenced Faraday rotation angles measured from GQD particles at 405 nm as a function of particle mass concentration. b) Total Verdet constants (solvent + particles) calculated from (a), referenced to the Verdet constant of water, V
H2
O = 8.84 rad T−1 m−1. The error bars are from three separate experiments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fitting of the Verdet constants to Equation (2), using F = A
FR ·f
0 ·(2π/c)4, and λ
0 = 2πc/ω
0 as the two fit parameters, for all GQD and N-GQD (Table 2). The measured Verdet constants are marked by filled circles, and the standard deviations are indicated with vertical error bars. The projection of each value on the horizontal plane is marked by a light-colored dot. GQD-45 could not be fitted. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Verdet constants V from fitted slopes (Fig. 3b) at the wavelengths of 405, 520, and 658 nm in all measured GQD and N-GQD samples. The standard deviations are from three separate experiments of the same sample.

row.entry: V (405 nm) rad T−1 m2 kg−1
V (520 nm) rad T−1 m2 kg−1
V (658 nm) rad T−1 m2 kg−1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Fitted F = A
FR ·f
0 ·(2π/c)4, and λ
0 = 2πc/ω
0 to equation V = F/(λ
0
−2−λ −2)2 corresponding to Equation (2), for all GQD and N-GQD samples. The errors indicated are determined using the Jacobian matrix from the fit.

head.ce:title: Large Faraday rotation in pyrolysis synthesized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, 77843, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Mexico Consortium, 4200 W. Jemez Rd, Suite 301, Los Alamos, NM, 87544, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: New Mexico Consortium, 4200 W. Jemez Rd, Suite 301, Los Alamos, NM 87544, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Pyrolysis of citric acid produced carbon dots on the 10 nm size scale, tunable by synthesis time. Magneto-optical spectroscopy revealed a size-dependent Faraday rotation. For particles with optimal synthesis time, the Verdet constant was measured as >300 times larger than that of water on a per-mass basis. Introducing ethylenediamine substrate as a nitrogen dopant in the synthesis resulted in a similar effect. These behaviors are indicative of extended sp2 networks in the particles. A strong wavelength dependence with largest rotation at the shortest measured wavelength of 405 nm was observed. This dependence is explained by a comparison with the theory of electron-photon interactions, which predicts an increase of the Verdet constant with 1/(ω
0
2-ω
2 )2 for an angular frequency ω and an absorption line at ω
0 . The fitting of the analytical function to the measured Faraday rotation indicates a wavelength near 300 nm corresponding to the ω
0 , consistent with an extended electron delocalization. Nanomaterials with large magneto-optical effects have new proposed technological applications, such as in their use as magnetic field sensors. The Faraday rotation is related to spin-induced effects, foremost nuclear spin optical rotation, suggesting further applications of these materials supporting an optical readout of spin states in quantum devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Magneto-optical properties of materials play an important role in applications ranging from molecular sensing and quantum computing to the development of spectroscopy for the measurement of electronic configurations of molecules and materials in ground or excited states. Broadly, magneto-optics refers to the influence of a magnetic field on the optical properties of materials [1]. For example, the Faraday effect, the most prominent of them, is the rotation of the polarization plane of a linearly polarized light beam propagating in a material exposed to an external magnetic field. Other well-known magneto-optical effects include magnetic circular dichroism (MCD) and magnetic birefringence where, similarly to the Faraday effect, the respective optical properties are induced by the presence of a magnetic field.

By modifying the polarization of an optical beam, the Faraday effect provides a means for remote magnetic field sensing, on length scales ranging from the microscopic to the astronomical [2,3]. In one application, the detection of the Faraday effect has been proposed for biological imaging in cells, using magnetic nanoparticles as contrast agents [4]. The Faraday effect has also been reported to serve as probe for acoustic behaviors of superfluid [5]. The Faraday effect and other analogous magneto-optical effects have been proposed and achieved detection of electron spin coherence information from quantum dot materials, constituting a strong competitor for quantum computing information readout [6,7]. Magnetism induced by an inverse Faraday effect from rapid laser pulses may be used for magnetic memory and circuit devices [8]. Other magneto-optical effects form the basis for different observations. MCD has been used in biology to measure oxidation states in metalloproteins [9]. Magnetic birefringence is proposed for the characterization of molecular assemblies forming nanostructures [10]. Because of the interrelation of magneto-optical effects, the characterization of the Faraday effect in diverse substrates will also support the discovery of new applications of these other effects [11].

The strength of the Faraday effect is characterized by the Verdet constant V, such that
(1)
Δ θ = V B l

Here, Δθ is the optical polarization rotation angle, B is the externally applied magnetic field parallel to the light beam, and l is the optical path length through the medium. Although the Verdet constant is frequently measured as an empirical parameter, it can be predicted from first principles by calculating the dynamic polarizability of electrons interacting with the light. The result indicates a strong dependence of the Verdet constant on the proximity to absorption lines in the spectrum. By neglecting some effects, such as broadening at large detuning from the absorption resonances, the Verdet constant can be estimated as [12].
(2)
V =
A
F R
∑ k
f k
(
ω k 2
−
ω 2
)
2

The angular frequency ω = 2πc/λ corresponds to a specific wavelength λ of probing light, related by the speed of light c, ω
k are the angular frequencies of absorption lines, f
k are their oscillator strengths, and A
FR is a constant of proportionality that in a liquid or molecular solid depends among other factors on the concentration of the molecules. For instance, the giant Faraday effect has been studied experimentally in a polymer containing magnetic nanoparticles [13] and theoretically in thin-film topological insulators [14]. Materials with giant Verdet constants can find many applications in miniaturized magnetic sensors.

The Faraday rotation in substances can be induced not only by an external field but also by internal fields due to polarized nuclear spins – the phenomenon termed nuclear spin optical rotation (NSOR) [15]. Having a similar wavelength dependence as the Faraday effect, in addition, NSOR depends on the strength of hyperfine interactions in the excited states. Owing to the local nature of the hyperfine interaction, this effect can form a basis for hybrid nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and optical spectroscopy that can provide information on the localization of the excited electronic states [16]. Similar to the Faraday effect, the NSOR depends on the path length l,
(3)
Δ θ =
φ
N S O R
C P l

The product of the nuclear spin concentration C and polarization P, to which the magnetic field generated by the nuclear spins is proportional, replaces the external magnetic field in Equation (1) for the Faraday rotation. The nuclear spin polarization is the difference between the nuclear spin-up and spin-down populations, divided by the total number of nuclear spins. The spin population distribution described by P is normally generated by an external magnetic field. In brief, The NSOR constant is approximately
(4)
φ
N S O R
=
A
N S O R
∑ k
f k
a k
(
ω k 2
−
ω 2
)
2
where a
k are the hyperfine coupling constants [17,18]. This expression neglects the effects of the finite resonance width, is valid away from the resonance, and is analogous to Equation (2).

In addition to NSOR, a Faraday effect caused by electron spin magnetic moments can also be measured in molecules (radicals) and atoms with unpaired spins. It was shown that time-averaged single electron spin coherence can be read from a semiconductor assembly [19]. Apart from optical polarization rotation, some nuclear spin-induced magneto-optical effects, such as birefringence, are predicted to exist but have to date not been measured [20].

Notably, different materials and molecules exhibit magneto-optical effects of widely varying magnitude. Solids such as crystals and semiconductors often show an unusually large Faraday rotation, whereby certain crystals find applications as optical modulators, isolators, or magnetic field sensors. Organic molecules, such as in solvents, generally exhibit smaller but nevertheless detectable Faraday and nuclear spin optical rotation [21–24].

Among carbon-based materials, graphene possesses an exceptionally large Verdet constant in the THz spectral region. This material has been proposed for applications in various magneto-optical devices [25], and for serving as a basis of THz-band absorption devices [26–28]. In addition to bulk materials, nanoparticles exhibit interesting features for new technological applications due to their modulation of bulk properties by quantum confinement effects [29]. A quantum Faraday effect was also discovered in graphene, similar to the quantum Hall effect [30]. The Faraday and nuclear spin optical rotation effects in graphene nanoparticles have, to date, only been studied theoretically. A strong dependence of the optical rotation properties on the particle size as well as composition, such as given by edge structure, was found [31].

Graphene particles can be synthesized in several ways [32,33]. Among these, the bottom-up pyrolysis synthesis starting from citric acid is a cost-effective method capable of producing particles of different composition, if required, with the inclusion of dopant atoms [34]. In this paper, we measure the Faraday rotation of pyrolysis synthesized graphene particles with and without nitrogen doping. We find a large Faraday rotation in particles produced with an optimal pyrolysis time, analyze these results based on the underlying theory, and propose their Faraday effect and NSOR based applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD samples
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene quantum dot (GQD) synthesis was modified from previously reported protocols [34,35]. A Teflon hydrothermal reaction container with 2 g of anhydrous citric acid substrate (Amresco, Solon, OH) was heated to 200 °C for a predetermined time of 45, 60, 75, or 90 min in an oven (Gr Con 3.7CF, VWR International, Radnor, PA). From this treatment, a viscous molten orange-to-brown liquid was obtained. This liquid was poured into 30 ml of cold aqueous solution of 1 M sodium hydroxide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) and vigorously stirred overnight. The pH of the solution was then neutralized by titration with 1 M hydrochloric acid (VWR International). The resulting brown solution was deionized by dialysis in cartridges (Pierce Slide-A-Lyzer, 10 kDa MWCO, 150–250 ml; Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA). A stable orange GQD solution was thus obtained. Samples of nitrogen-doped GQD (N-GQD) were synthesized with the same method, except that the 2 g of citric acid were mixed with 540 μL ethylenediamine (VWR International) and let cool to 298 K while covered. The cover was removed before placing the mixture in the preheated oven.

The GQD and N-GQD samples were characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The UV–vis spectra were measured in a spectrophotometer (BioSpec-mini, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were acquired with a spectrophotometer (QuantaMaster40, Photon Technology International, Edison, NJ). The Raman spectra were obtained with a Raman Spectrometer (i-Raman, B&W Tek, Plainsboro, NJ) with a 785 nm laser. The AFM images were obtained with an AFM microscope (nanoIR2-s, Bruker, Billerica, MA). The TEM images were obtained with a TEM microscope (Titan Themis 300 S, FEI Company, Hillsboro, OR). The mass concentrations of the stock aqueous solution used in the experiments were ascertained from the dry weight after lyophilization.

A spectrometer was designed to measure the Faraday rotation of the aqueous solutions at different wavelengths (Fig. 1 a). A red 658 nm (L658P040, Thorlabs, Newton, NJ), green 520 nm (L520P50, Thorlabs), or blue 405 nm (L4-405M-100-TE, Micro Laser System Inc., Garden Grove, CA) laser was employed. The Thorlabs laser diodes were mounted on a mount (LDM56, Thorlabs) and controlled by a laser current (LDC205C, Thorlabs) and a temperature controller (TED200C, Thorlabs). The laser diodes were kept at 298 K and working at powers of 40 mW (the red laser) or 50 mW (the green and blue lasers). The laser beams were reflected by a mirror (P100A, Newport, Irvine, CA) for beam direction adjustment and polarized by a linear polarizer (WPH10E-405, Thorlabs). The beam passed through a 1 cm long sample-containing quartz cuvette (110-10-40, Hellma, Plainview, NY) into a beam splitter cube (PBS121, Thorlabs). The intensities of the two split beams were then measured separately by two photodiodes (FGAP71, Thorlabs). With two identical trans-impedance amplifiers, the electrical current outputs from the photodiodes were converted into voltages, and then with a difference operational amplifier (OPA4131PJ, Texas Instruments, Dallas, TX) configured to have a unity gain, were subtracted to reduce laser technical noise [24]. The voltage signal of one photodiode was additionally recorded to calibrate the laser intensity and to determine the absorbance of each liquid sample (Fig. S9). In order to improve the sensitivity of the measurement, the difference signal was amplified 10,000 times with a voltage preamplifier (SR560, Stanford Research Systems, Sunnyvale, CA) and filtered with a 100 Hz high-pass and a 1000 Hz low-pass filter (filter gain of 0.864 at 500 Hz). In the Faraday rotation measurements, a Helmholtz coil with 60 turns per side and 10 cm diameter produced a magnetic field parallel to the laser beam in the cuvette from an alternating current of frequency of 500 Hz and a duration of 3 s. These pulses were generated by an analog output channel of a data acquisition board (PCIe-6363, National Instruments, Austin, TX) and amplified by an audio amplifier (TPA3118). In addition to delivering a current to the coil, the voltage of the amplifier output was recorded for magnetic field monitoring to improve the accuracy of the Verdet constant calculations from the Faraday rotation. The typical voltage amplitude was measured at 3.78 V. The Helmholtz coil had a measured impedance of 3.03 + 4.30i Ω, from which an estimated field of B
est = 0.776 mT amplitude was calculated. Since we measured relative Faraday rotations with respect to water samples, an accurate absolute measurement of the magnetic field was not necessary. Data acquisition was performed using the same data acquisition board that generated the pulses, and the detected signal was synchronous with the excitation field. For each sample, 25 averages were taken to improve accuracy.

The Faraday rotation was measured from GQD samples synthesized with 45, 60, 75, and 90 min pyrolysis times (GQD-45, GQD-60, GQD-75, and GQD-90, respectively), as well as N-GQD samples produced using the same synthesis times (N-GQD-45, N-GQD-60, N-GQD-75, and N-GQD-90, respectively). Three data acquisitions were performed for each sample. Every signal acquisition was accompanied by a control acquisition, where the laser was turned off. The obtained time domain data was stored and processed using code written in Python (Python Software Foundation, https://www.python.org/) [36]. The data processing included a Fourier transform to determine the amplitude of the signal corresponding to the excitation frequency of 500 Hz. The frequency domain analysis took the place of the lock-in detection in a traditional instrument, allowing the use of general-purpose data acquisition hardware [37]. The spectrum of the experiment with the laser turned off was subtracted to remove contributions from background signals and electrical crosstalk. The integral of the signal within a 20 Hz frequency range was divided by the average reference signal from one of the photodiodes, V
single , and the preamplifier gain to calculate the rotation angle. The angle was calculated as Δθ = (V
diff −V
dark )/(4·gain·V
single ). For the determination of the Verdet constant, Δθ was divided by the integral of the Fourier-transformed reference signal of the coil voltage at 500 Hz and fitted as Δθ/V
field = m·C + b. V
field is the reference voltage signal from the audio amplifier (Fig. 1b). The fit parameters m and b were used to calculate the total Verdet constant of the sample solution by scaling the fitted line for the absolute rotation angle so the intercept became equal to the Verdet constant of water at the corresponding wavelength, V
t = n·(m·C + b) with n = V
H
2
O /b. The reference values for the Verdet constants of water, V H20, were taken as 8.84, 5.12, and 3.05 rad T−1 m−1 at 405, 520, and 658 nm, respectively [38]. The density of water at room temperature of 997.0 kg m−3 was used to calculate the corresponding Verdet constants per unit density: 8.87 × 10−3, 5.14 × 10−3 and 3.06 × 10−3 rad T−1 m2 kg−1, at wavelengths of 405, 520 and 658 nm, respectively. Separately, the total Verdet constant can be estimated using the approximate magnetic field value as V
t,est = Δθ/(B
est ·l). Δθ was calculated to range from 0.0650 to 0.0675 mrad. Using B
est = 0.776 mT and l = 0.01 m, V
t,est at 405 nm ranges from 8.38 to 8.70 rad T−1 m−1. The measurement and analysis methods were validated by the determination of Verdet constants from different solvents (Fig. S1).

The GQD and N-GQD samples were characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), absorption/emission spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The AFM images (Fig. 2 a–d, Fig. S5) showed the average sizes of the GQD and N-GQD particle. A linear regression was performed to correlate the size parameters and the pyrolysis time (Fig. S6 and Table S2). The particle widths were fitted to slopes of 1.43 ± 0.43 and 0.70 ± 0.30 nm per minute of pyrolysis for GQD and N-GQD, respectively, and the height to slopes of 0.066 ± 0.030 and 0.0258 ± 0.0010 nm per minute, respectively. While the slopes indicate the size correlation, the growth of the particles should not be interpreted as a linear temporal process. The results demonstrate that the width and thickness increased with extended pyrolysis time, and that the synthesized carbon dots are polydisperse. The GQD and N-GQD were identified to have nanometer-scale size and 2–12 and 3–9 layers of graphene sheets, considering the average 0.335 nm interlayer spacing [39]. The TEM images (Fig. S7) showed a few-layered and highly ordered hexagonal lamellar lattice structure of GQD-60. Its atomic spacing was determined to be 0.151 nm by local two-dimensional Fourier transform (2DFFT) of the TEM images, demonstrating an sp2 hybridized region throughout the undoped sample. The N-GQD-60 sample showed a less ordered and a warped form, nevertheless allowing the determination of a 0.147 nm atomic spacing and 0.371 nm interlayer spacing by local 2DFFT. The size and thickness match with previously reported few-layered graphene quantum dots synthesized from pyrolysis of citric acid [40]. The N-GQD absorption spectra showed 350 nm and 205 nm absorption peaks, indicating the presence of sp2 nitrogen dopant and leftover hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Fig. 2i). The GQD absorption spectra showed a 240 nm absorption peak, as well as a strong peak at a shorter wavelength, which are also attributed to sp2 carbon regions [41]. The sp2 nature was also buttressed by the Raman spectra (Fig. S10), which showed a consistent strong G-band centered at 1608 cm−1 as a result of the characteristic symmetrical stretching of graphitic-like carbon [42]. The integration of the mass-concentration-normalized G-band peaks increases with pyrolysis time (Fig. S11), which can be readily argued as a result of continuous growth of the graphene quantum dots at pyrolysis conditions. Additionally, a weak D-band centered at 1340 cm−1 may be observable close to the noise level, which would indicate the presence of defects or symmetry breaking [43]. The emission spectra (Fig. S8) showed a varying maximum emission wavelength at varying excitation wavelength, also confirming the polydispersity of the particle size [41].

The Faraday rotation of GQD particles measured as a function of concentration in aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 3 a. The slopes of linear fits give the Faraday rotation by the particles normalized per their densities. The linearity of each data set confirms the stability of the particles and the accuracy of the concentrations in the dilution series. The intercept values in the Δθ at zero concentration, corresponding to the Faraday rotation of water, showed a measurement error of approximately 5 % based on the spread of these values. In Fig. 3b, the calibrated data was referenced to the reported Verdet constant of water at 405 nm. Thus, the intercepts in all the shown measurements became equal to the reported Verdet constant.

From the slope of the curves in Fig. 3b, it is seen that some of the GQD and N-GQD samples showed a strong Faraday rotation compared to water, resulting in an increase in the total Verdet constant of nearly 30 % despite a very low concentration of the particles of only approximately 1:1000 by mass.

The Verdet constants of the samples were determined from the fitted slopes (Table 1
). In addition to the data at 405 nm, the measurements at 520 nm and 658 nm are included in the table. The mean values and standard deviations of the Verdet constants were calculated from three separate experiments with the same sample. The measured Verdet constants ranged from 24.4 to 314 times that of water at all measured wavelengths, excluding GQD-45 and N-GQD-45, for which the Verdet constants were too small to measure reliably. Assuming a 680 kg m−3 mass density of the GQD [44], a Verdet constant as large as 1900, 970 and 440 rad T−1 m−1 on a volumetric basis could be extrapolated for the pure material at 405, 520 and 658 nm, respectively. In comparison, the Verdet constants of two widely commercialized materials, terbium gallium garnet [45] and terbium aluminum garnet [46] were measured to be 122.4–155.5 and 172.72 rad T−1 m−1 at 632.8 nm, respectively. The Verdet constant of the GQD is shown comparable to various films of organic molecules with strong Zeeman splitting of the degenerate ground or excited electronic states [47]. This property is common to high-symmetry molecules with aromatic cores, which could explain the strong Faraday rotation of GQD.

The Faraday rotation was found to be the smallest for GQD-45, moderate for GQD-90, and the largest for GQD-60 and GQD-75. With nitrogen dopant, the N-GQD-45 and N-GQD-90 showed moderate rotations, and N-GQD-60 and N-GQD-75 had the largest rotations. In light of previous theoretical studies of the size dependence of the graphene quantum dots that reported a positive correlation of the Verdet constant to the sp2 carbon region size [31], the pyrolysis time dependence of the Faraday rotation was attributed to the varying sp2 hybridized region size. An insufficient pyrolysis time, in the case of GQD-45 and N-GQD-45, resulted in a smaller Faraday rotation, as the substrates were yet to complete polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization to produce and connect sp2 carbon regions. The carbonization eventually stops as substrates and oligomers are consumed. It can be hypothesized that a subsequent reaction between moieties beyond layers would slowly take place and lead to aggregation, thus deforming or shrinking the existing sp2 region. This effect would eventually lead to a reduction in the Verdet constant, consistent with the observation of a maximum at 60–75 min of synthesis time.

An empirical extrapolation of the Verdet constants as a function of wavelength was used to compare the graphene quantum dots to small organic molecules, predominantly solvents. An equation V = A + B·λ −2 was used for fitting (Figs. S2–S4; Table S1) [38,48]. A λ −4 term present in the literature was truncated to avoid overfitting. According to our analysis, this term contributed to less than 7 % to the Verdet constant at the experimental wavelengths in water. The comparison indicated that the graphene quantum dots synthesized with an optimal reaction time had a B-coefficient approximately two orders of magnitude larger than the small molecules. This observation reflects the large Faraday rotation induced by the graphene quantum dots near the absorbance line.

Equation (2), which is based on the atomic theory of the electron-photon interaction, can also be used to fit the wavelength dependence of the Faraday rotation measurements to specific absorption lines (Fig. 4 ). We limited our analysis to two fitting parameters assuming one dominant or composite line: λ
0
= λ
k and A
FR ·f
0 , due to a limited number of wavelength points, namely three. From the fits, we found that the λ
0 value ranges from 270 to 390 nm for all samples that were fitted. The reported and currently measured absorption and emission peak ranges agree with the range of fitted λ
k values, displaying a consistency of the absorption spectrum and the Faraday rotation [41].

Based on these characterizations of particle size, the measured Faraday rotations may be compared to the predictions of the aforementioned computational study [31]. The measured Faraday rotations would be consistent with particles larger than approximately those of the third-generation particles in the computational study, i.e., larger than 2.9 nm. This size range would be smaller than the particle sizes observed in AFM, indicating that the extent of the sp2 regions is smaller than the entire particle, possibly existing as islands separated by sp3 carbons. On the other hand, comparing the experimental Faraday rotation values to the theoretical prediction for a similar particle size indicates that the Verdet constant by mass concentration of the GQD could still be increased by one order of magnitude. Such improvements may be realized in the absence of structural defects as in ideal graphene. The theoretical study also found a strong dependence of the Faraday rotation on the structure of the particles, particularly the edge composition. Such effects may render a quantitative comparison between simulated and measured values difficult. Different structural features may also play a role in the Faraday rotation of the graphene quantum dots observed here, leading to the differences between the undoped and nitrogen-doped particles.

The large Faraday rotation of the graphene quantum dots observed here may lend itself to several applications. While a single graphene layer was observed to exhibit an unusually large Verdet constant in the terahertz spectral region [25], the nanoparticles with absorbance in the UV/visible spectral region can be detected with more conventional optical methods using lasers for better polarization measurement sensitivity. Nanoparticles could be integrated into devices, for instance, by deposition as thin films to achieve a high density in the solid state [49] or encapsulation of lyophilized particles reconstituted in concentrated solutions, in ways similar to liquid crystal displays [50]. It can be predicted that the GQD particles would retain the large Faraday rotation in the solid state because the magneto-optical properties originate from the electronic states of the particle. A large Faraday rotation in the solid state would enable the application of the GQD in solid state devices similar to paramagnetic ion centers of commercial Faraday materials, for instance, garnet crystals [51,52]. One potential application of GQD can be magnetic microscopy, where an increase of the Faraday rotation can be translated into higher magnetic-field sensitivity. While significant absorption normally limits the maximum concentration because the path length cannot exceed the absorption length by much, in micro-magnetic applications, the path length can be very small, for example, 0.1 mm. With simple synthesis and low cost of lanthanide-free substrates, GQD could become an economical alternative for Faraday materials currently in use.

Because of the close relation of the Faraday effect to spin-based magneto-optical effects, NSOR in particular, the Verdet constant is one indicator of the magnitude of the latter. A large Verdet constant, if combined with favorable magnetic resonance properties, shows promise for applications based on spin-induced optical rotation. These applications include the fundamental study of excited state electron configurations in the carbon-based materials using a hybrid magnetic resonance-optical spectroscopy or the non-invasive imaging of nuclear spin dynamics by light without perturbation by field gradients [15]. Quantum materials with GQD-like structures may in the future serve as platforms for quantum devices that rely on nuclear spin states. One advantage of Carbon-13 nuclei that have a spin of ½ is a long decoherence time. The pyrolysis synthesis method would easily allow doping of the graphene framework with this isotope. Quantum information encoded in 13C spin coherences may be read out through the large nuclear spin induced Faraday effect.

In summary, we found that graphene quantum dots made with an optimal synthesis time exhibit a large Faraday rotation, several hundred times that of water and small organic molecules. The observed Faraday rotation can be attributed to extended regions of sp2 carbon. These regions appear to be present in the particles synthesized from citric acid alone and in particles that include nitrogen doping. While a large Faraday rotation of graphene in the THz spectral region was previously known, Faraday rotation in the UV/visible range in inexpensively synthesized graphene quantum dots was observed for the first time. Because of the convenient wavelength range, which can be covered with conventional lasers, several possible applications in sensing and quantum information science can be potentially anticipated. Particularly, the extension of Faraday rotation to spin-based effects, including NSOR, which is based on magnetic fields internal to the material produced by nuclear spins, would be of interest for the optical readout of spin quantum states.

Zefan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Igor Savukov: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Christian Hilty: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Science Foundation (CHE-2108691 to I.S. and CHE-2108822 to C.H.) and the Welch Foundation (A-1658 to C.H.) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Prof. Juha Vaara for useful discussions. Assistance with AFM from Dr. Wilson Serem and TEM from Dr. Sisi Xiang of the Texas A&M University Materials Characterization Facility (RRID: SCR_022202) is greatly appreciated, as well as assistance with Raman spectroscopy from Mr. Justin Smolen of the Texas A&M University Laboratory for Synthetic-Biologic Interactions (RRID: SCR_022287).
